A few wooden barrels or crates are visible near this light source.)
(The location is now Dimhollow Crypt.) (Characters nearby (from nearest to furthest): Daegon, rabbit, rabbit, rabbit, Anna)

Lydia: Another tomb. These Nords sure knew how to bury their dead deep. I hope we find something worth the chill creeping up my spine. Jenassa: The dead should stay dead. These crypts always feel like a trap waiting to spring. At least the sky above is clear—a small mercy before we descend further.

Jenassa: The aurora dances tonight. Purple and pink like spilled arcane essence across the sky. Even a mercenary can pause for such a sight. Lydia: Aye, it's beautiful. But beautiful things in Skyrim have a way of distracting you from the blades aimed at your back. Still... I'll admit, standing here under the lights with the snow at our feet, it feels like the gods are watching. Jenassa: Dibella's handiwork, no doubt. She favors those who appreciate beauty even in the midst of death's work. Lydia: Dibella favors those who fight for what's theirs. And these crypts? They're ours now. Whatever's inside, we'll claim it for the Dragonborn.

Lydia: By Ysmir, they're everywhere! Jenassa, watch your flank! Jenassa: I see them! That firebolt nearly took my head off—watch your aim, Daegon! Lydia: Solvi's got a fancy sword. I'll split her skull for it! Come on, you walking bones! Jenassa: Death hounds and skeletons... I hate fighting things that don't know when to stay dead. Keep moving, don't let them surround us! Lydia: The Dragonborn's arrows are singing tonight! Keep shooting, my Thane—we'll clean up the scraps!

Lydia: Filthy archers, hiding in the shadows. I hope that Nathalie's bowstring snaps the next time she draws it. At least the death hounds are put down for good. Jenassa: The skeletons crumble, the hounds fall silent, and yet I still feel uneasy. This place has a hunger about it. We should find what the Dragonborn came for and leave before something worse awakens. Lydia: Agreed. My greatsword is thirsty for more than bones and dogs. Let's push deeper and see what else this crypt hides. The Dragonborn's arrows guided us true tonight—I'll follow where they lead.

Lydia: This crypt feels wrong. Even the air tastes old and bitter. I'd rather face a dozen bandits in the open than spend another hour in these stone halls. Jenassa: The dead cling to their treasures here. I can feel their resentment in every draft. The sooner we find whatever the Dragonborn seeks, the sooner we can breathe fresh air again. Lydia: Aye. And if more skeletons or death hounds crawl out, I'll send them back to Oblivion piece by piece. Jenassa: Save your strength, Lydia.

Lydia: My Thane asks a good question. Vampires skulking in a crypt this remote... they're after something specific. A weapon, perhaps, or a pact sealed in blood. Nords don't bury secrets lightly. Jenassa: Whatever it is, the dead here were meant to guard it. I've heard whispers of an ancient vampire prophecy—a return of something older than the Jarls' histories. These ruins may hold the key. Lydia: Prophecy or not, we'll cut through whatever stands between us and the truth. Lead on, Dragonborn. Let's see what these stone walls are hiding.
(Characters nearby (from nearest to furthest): Daegon, Bea, Skeleton, Skeleton, Death Hound) (MJR equipped Daedric Arrow)
(The location is now Dimhollow Crypt.) (Characters nearby (from nearest to furthest): Jenassa, Skeleton, Bea, Skeleton, Skeleton)

Daegon: I hate crypts. They always smell like old dust and regret. At least the skeletons give us something to smash. Lydia: Aye, but at least these bones don't talk back like some people I know. Watch your fire, elf—I don't want my bra singed off by another stray spell. Daegon: Oh please, your bra is the least of your worries in a place like this. The real question is what's waiting deeper in—treasure, traps, or something worse. Lydia: Something worse usually means vampires. Or worse, Thalmor agents hiding in the dark. I'd rather fight a hundred skeletons than one of those golden-skinned pricks. Daegon: Funny you mention Thalmor. They'd probably hate this place too—too much dust for their pristine robes. But I suppose a crypt is as good a place as any to bury secrets. Lydia: Secrets are for bards and spies. Give me a clear foe and an open field. This cramped tunnel makes my greatsword feel like a toothpick.

Lydia: Good work with that firebolt, elf. You set that skeleton's bones crackling like dry timber. Though I still say you nearly singed my hair off with that one. Daegon: Your hair is the least of my concerns when that Bea woman turned MJR into a goat or something with that Wabbajack. Did you see him stagger? Lucky it wasn't worse. Lydia: Aye, I saw. Wabbajack is a madman's toy. Sheikah nonsense from the Shivering Isles. At least she's dead now. One less bandit slut trying to capture our thane.

Daegon: That staff... the Wabbajack. I've read about it in the Arcane University archives. Sheogorath's plaything. To think a common bandit got her hands on something like that. Makes you wonder what else is lurking in these ruins. Lydia: Wabbajack or no, she's dead and we're not. That's what matters. Though I'll admit, seeing MJR wobble like a newborn deer was almost worth the fright. Almost. Daegon: You're too practical, Lydia. Don't you ever wonder about the power in these old stones?

Lydia: Ouch! By Dibella's tits, Daegon! Are you trying to cook me alive? Watch where you throw those damn firebolts! Daegon: Sorry, sorry! There's a death hound snapping at my heels and a draugr with a rusted axe—I'm a little busy! At least you're still standing, you big Nord ox. Lydia: I'll be standing long enough to shove my greatsword up that Wanheda's backside if she shows her face. Now focus! More bones rattling in the shadows!

Lydia: That gate looks old. Solid Nord craftsmanship, though. Probably been sealing something in for centuries. Daegon: Or sealing something out. Either way, it's not going to open for polite conversation. Do you see a lever or a chain anywhere? Lydia: I see nothing but shadow and stone. Maybe MJR can pick the lock, or we find another way around. I don't fancy standing here waiting for more draugr to wake up.

Daegon: The early morning light won't reach us down here, but I can feel it above. The air shifts, even this deep. Dawn's breaking over Skyrim while we rot in this stone tomb. Lydia: Better to rot in a tomb than freeze on a mountainside. At least the dead don't bite as hard as the wind. Still, I'd rather be marching through the Rift right now, watching the sun catch the autumn leaves. Daegon: The Rift? You surprise me, Lydia. I thought you preferred the frozen plains of Whiterun. Lydia: A Nord can appreciate beauty without living in it. The Rift is pretty enough to look at, but give me solid ground and open sky for a fight any day. Now come on—let's find whatever treasure or trap lies behind this gate before my feet get cold.

(The location is now Dimhollow Crypt.) (Characters nearby (from nearest to furthest): Restless Draugr)
Daegon: Ugh, another crypt, another pile of bones that refuse to stay dead. At least these draugr don't spin webs. Jenassa: Earthen passages do not agree with me.

Jenassa: Still water like this hides many things. Tread carefully on that plank, it may be the only thing between us and a cold grave. Daegon: Lovely. A flooded crypt with a walkway made of rotting timber. What's next, a troll playing a lute?

Lydia: Fine shot, my thane. That arrow echoed off the stone like a war drum. The dead will know we're coming now. Daegon: Let them know. I'd rather face a screaming draugr than a silent one sneaking up from behind.

Jenassa: The dead know we are here already. They have known since we set foot in this tomb. The question is whether we find what we seek before they rouse in force. Daegon: And what exactly are we seeking in this sunken hole anyway? Feels like we're chasing ghosts and old bones.

Lydia: We seek whatever the Dragonborn seeks. My oath is to his blade, not to question his purpose. Though I'll admit, this place has the stench of something ancient and foul. Daegon: Ancient and foul describes half of Skyrim. The other half is just cold and miserable.

Lydia: Enough talk. This crypt won't explore itself, and I'd rather meet whatever waits ahead on my feet than standing here in cold water. Jenassa: Agreed. Whatever prize lies deeper, I want it found before the sun fully rises. Daegon: Fine, fine. Lead on. Just try not to step on any pressure plates that drop us into a spike pit. I left my lucky coin at home. Jenassa: Stay sharp and stay quiet. Let us finish this.

Daegon: This place is too quiet. Even the draugr are holding their breath. Lydia: Aye, it's the kind of quiet that makes my knuckles itch for a hilt.

Lydia: I would rather be out in the open, splitting a bandit's shield in two than creeping through these damp halls. Daegon: Spoken like a true Nord. But out there, we'd have the sun on our faces and no skeletons to worry about. Well, fewer skeletons. Lydia: You say that like fighting in sunlight is any different. It is all the same to me. A swing is a swing whether under Dibella's sky or in this tomb. Daegon: Dibella's sky? I thought you worshipped the god of war, or whatever it is you Nords pray to when you're not sharpening your blade. Lydia: I honor the Divines. Especially Dibella. Beauty and strength go hand in hand. A well-forged greatsword is beautiful. A clean kill is beautiful. You should try seeing the grace in it. Daegon: I see plenty of grace. Mostly when I set things on fire. There is a certain elegance to a well-aimed firebolt. Lydia: Fire is just messy. It leaves nothing but ash and stink. A proper sword leaves a man knowing he was bested.

Daegon: Look at that. A body in the middle of the path. And those webs are fresh enough to glisten. Lydia: Master vampire nesting ground, if I had to guess. They like to keep their larders close. Daegon: I hate spiders. And vampires. And this whole crypt. But at least the vampires burn nicely. Lydia: Aye, they do. But watch yourself. That gate up ahead looks like it could seal us in if we are not careful. Daegon: Then we burn through it. Or find a clever way around. I am open to suggestions that do not involve getting trapped. Lydia: Clever is your job. Cleaving is mine. Let us see what is behind that door before anything else crawls out of the dark.

Lydia: By Ysmir's beard, Daegon! Are you trying to roast the Dragonborn alive? Watch where you throw those firebolts! Daegon: It was an accident! That spider was right in my line of sight and the arrow went wide. At least we are both still standing. Lydia: Barely. My arm still stings from that poison. But I will take a few spider bites over a face full of your magic any day. Daegon: You are welcome for the help. Next time I will just let the spider have you and see if you prefer its method of dispatch. Lydia: Ha! You would miss me too much. Who else would carry all the treasure we find? Besides, the Dragonborn would be disappointed if you turned me into a crispy Nord. Daegon: True enough. Now come on. That gate is still ahead, and I want to see what light is shining through it before something else finds us.

Daegon: More vampires! And a flame atronach? Someone up there is having a party without us. Lydia: Lokil! I recognise that name from the Dawnguard reports. He is one of Harkon's lieutenants. Daegon: Fancy title for a bloodsucker. I wonder if he burns as well as the rest. Lydia: Let us find out. Keep that fire handy, elf. I am going to split his skull. Daegon: Try not to get yourself killed. I would hate to have to explain to the Dragonborn why I let you die. Lydia: Do not worry about me. Worry about keeping up.

Daegon: Well, Lokil is no more. He put up a decent fight, I will give him that. Lydia: Aye, but he fell like the rest. Another leech put down. Good work with that fire, elf. Even if you did singe my hair a little. Daegon: You are welcome. And I aim to please. Now then, that mysterious woman is still standing. Let us see what she wants before we find more trouble. Lydia: Agreed. But keep your spells ready. I do not trust anyone in this crypt.

Jenassa: Another tomb, another collection of restless bones. I’d almost rather face a bear than these walking corpses. Serana: Bears don’t usually have glowing eyes and a grudge about their funeral arrangements. I find the dead far more entertaining—they never bore you with talk about the weather.

Serana: Well, that’s not something you see every day. A glowing purple altar with a dead tree growing out of it—very chic. I wonder if the carrots are a ritual offering or someone’s forgotten lunch. Jenassa: This place reeks of old magic. Not the subtle kind, either. I’ve seen Dwemer ruins with less nonsense than this. Best keep your blade ready. Lydia: I don’t like it. Pretty lights and dead trees mean trouble. If something rises from that thing, I’m putting my greatsword through it first. Dibella guide our strikes.

Serana: Five skeletons shuffling our way—looks like the altar sent a welcoming committee. Hope you’re ready to crack some old bones, Lydia. Lydia: Let them come. I’ll split them like firewood and Dibella can admire the splinters. Jenassa: I’ll take the ones in the back. Don’t block my shots with that greatsword of yours. And MJR, if you’re grabbing potions, save me one—I might need it after this mess.

Serana: Ah, nothing clears the head like a morning bone-splintering. Though I do hope that altar is more interesting than these skeletons—they didn’t even put up a decent fight. Lydia: Their skulls cracked easy enough. But that purple altar still stands. I’d rather smash it than wonder what it does. Jenassa: Leave it. We’re not here to dismantle every enchanted relic. MJR has the right idea—grab what’s useful and move on. I’d rather breathe fresh air than tempt a curse.

Serana: And just like that, another one bites the dust. Or the cobblestone, in this case. Who was that, a stray bandit or a local welcoming committee? Jenassa: Didn’t catch her name. Didn’t need to. She picked the wrong target, and now she’s nothing but a memory. Lydia: She fought with desperation, not skill. A coward’s blade. Dibella favors those who stand firm, not those who skulk in shadows. Jenassa: Skulking worked fine for me. She never saw my dagger until it was too late.

Serana: Well, that was a proper brawl. I see you summoned your pet gargoyle again, MJR. Quite the party trick—though I’m still not sure if it’s helping or just getting in the way. Lydia: Gargoyle or no, we’re still standing and they’re dust. That’s what matters. Though I could do without another skeleton’s sword in my ribs. Jenassa: We’re all bleeding a little. That draugr had a mean swing. But the crypt’s quieter now, and that’s all I care about. Let’s find whatever we came for and get out.

Serana: You know, Lydia, there's something almost cozy about this crypt. The skeletons really know how to keep a place drafty and depressing. I'd say it's a fixer-upper, but the neighbors are dead. Lydia: Cozy? This place reeks of old death and stale magic. Dibella's tits, I'd rather be splitting Orc skulls in an open field than crawling through this tomb again. At least out there I can see my enemy coming. Serana: Oh, but where's the mystery in that? Nothing gets the blood flowing like wondering if the next corner holds a draugr with a grudge or a trap that'll drop a ceiling on your pretty blonde head. Speaking of, nice work with that greatsword back there. You really know how to make bones fly. Lydia: Flattery won't get you out of carrying your share of the loot, vampire. And don't think I didn't notice you hanging back when that frostbite spider dropped from the ceiling. Too busy thinking about fucking that summoned gargoyle again? Serana: Jealous that I have options? Mortal men, immortal beasts—variety is the spice of eternal life.

Serana: Oh, look, the local welcoming committee has arrows now. How thoughtful of them to announce their presence. Lydia: Dibella's grace, that one had my name on it! Lucky it just grazed my shoulder. Watch the archway, MJR's sending that gargoyle through! Serana: At least your gargoyle is good for something besides lurking and drooling. Though between you and me, I'd rather have him trap that skeleton archer in a corner. Lydia: Keep talking, vampire, and I'll let the next arrow hit you instead. Now move! I want to get close enough to split that bow in half.

Serana: Ow! That skeleton has terrible aim but good persistence. Lydia, are you still standing or did you decide to take a nap? Lydia: I'm fine! Just a scratch from that bitch Karla Raven. Dibella, give me strength to reach her and shove this greatsword where the sun don't shine. Serana: Such poetry. You should write a song about it. Meanwhile, MJR is picking them off one by one. That Daedric bow of his is a work of art. Lydia: He's showing off again. I love it when he does that. Now if only this gargoyle would stop getting in my way! Serana: Be nice to the gargoyle. He's trying his best.

Serana: Well, that was invigorating. Nothing like a bit of arrow dodging to wake you up in the morning. Did you see MJR shout that skeleton into the wall? Beautiful. Lydia: Beautiful indeed. That Thu'um of his sends them flying like ragdolls. Dibella smiles on his aim today. Shame about Karla Raven getting away, though. I wanted to teach her some manners. Serana: She'll turn up again. Bandits always do. Probably nursing a bruised ego and a bruised ass from that Fus she caught. Lydia: Good.

Serana: Well, well, well. Looks like Karla Raven won't be bothering anyone again. I'd say rest in peace, but she's probably warming a spot in Oblivion by now. Lydia: Good riddance. That bitch had it coming since she first drew a bow on us. Dibella's justice is swift. I hope she enjoys whatever Daedra she worships now. Serana: You know, for a goddess of beauty and desire, you sure invoke her name a lot when splitting skulls. Lydia: Dibella teaches that beauty can be found in a clean kill and a righteous death. That woman was neither beautiful nor righteous. Serana: Fair point. Now, shall we see if she had anything worth taking?

Serana: I swear, MJR could hit a skeever's left testicle from three hundred paces with that bow. Karla never stood a chance. Lydia: He's a true Nord shot. None of that elf-fancy archery, just pure skill and a steady hand. Makes me proud to fight at his side. Serana: Proud and soaking wet, I imagine. Lydia: Watch your tongue, vampire, or I'll show you what this greatsword can do to more than just skeletons.
